coredata.dc:title: A strong blue fluorescent nanoprobe based on Mg/N co-doped carbon dots coupled with molecularly imprinted polymer for ultrasensitive and highly selective detection of tetracycline in animal-derived foods
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Xuelian
Zhao, Yueqing
Dong, Jingyuan
Liu, Chang
Qi, Ying
Fang, Guozhen
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: Tetracycline (TC) plays a considerable role in the fight against microbial diseases. However, over-use of TC can produce serious harmful effects on humans and even may lead to drug tolerance. Herein, we developed a novel sensitive fluorescent nanosensor based on the encapsulation of magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Mg,N-CDs) into molecularly imprinted polymer (Mg,N-CDs@MIP), which can be utilized for the specific recognition and ultrasensitive detection of TC in animal-derived foods. The high luminescent carbon dots with a maximum emission wavelength of 455 nm under 350 nm excitation and a 33.05 % quantum yield were firstly synthesized using the Mg/N co-doped fluorescence-enhanced strategy, and the introduction of Mg,N-CDs as a signal tag can significantly enhance the sensitivity of fluorescent sensor. Then, via a facile and efficient one-pot reaction, the Mg,N-CDs@MIP was obtained. The resulting Mg,N-CDs@MIP not only displayed superior optical stability and good reusability, but also exhibited higher specificity and selectivity to TC compared with non-imprinted polymer (Mg,N-CDs@NIP) and Mg,N-CDs. The developed sensor was applied for determination of TC, and its fluorescence intensity was linearly quenched with the increasing concentration of TC from 5–100 ng mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.79 ng mL−1. Additionally, the proposed sensor was utilized for trace analysis of TC in complex foods, and the satisfactory results obtained were consistent with those obtained by HPLC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mg/N co-doped carbon dots
Tetracycline
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Animal-derived foods

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Mg,N-CDs@MIP and Mg,N-CDs@NIP composites
Preparation and characterization of Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor
Absorption behavior of Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor for TC
Establishment of fluorescence sensing detection method

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (project No. 2017YFC1600803 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation route of Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor.

SEM images of Mg,N-CDs@MIP (A) and Mg,N-CDs@NIP (C), inset: corresponding diameter distribution. TEM images of Mg,N-CDs@MIP (B) and Mg,N-CDs@NIP (D). (E) FT-IR spectra of Mg,N-CDs@NIP (curve a) and Mg,N-CDs@MIP (curve b). (F) TGA curves of Mg,N-CDs, Mg,N-CDs@MIP and Mg,N-CDs@NIP.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Mg,N-CDs and Mg,N-CDs@MIP under 350 nm excitation. The concentration of Mg,N-CDs and Mg,N-CDs@MIP was 0.25 mg mL−1. (B) Effect of pH values (3-12) on the fluorescence intensity of Mg,N-CDs@MIP and on the fluorescence quenching rate (F0/F) of Mg,N-CDs@MIP. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of Mg,N-CDs@NIP, Mg,N-CDs@MIP, and Mg,N-CDs@MIP combined with TC. (D) UV absorption spectral of Mg,N-CDs and TC, fluorescence emission spectrum of Mg,N-CDs@MIP and excitation spectrum of Mg,N-CDs@MIP.

(A) Fluorescence quenching degree of Mg,N-CDs@MIP, N-CDs@MIP and Mg,N-CDs@NIP toward different TC concentrations. (B) Fluorescence response of Mg,N-CDs@MIP and N-CDs@MIP at different contact times. The concentration of the above polymers was 0.25 mg mL−1.

(A) The chemical structures of tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), chloroamphenicol (CAP) and enrofloxacin (ENR). (B) The changes in fluorescence intensity of Mg,N-CDs@MIP, Mg,N-CDs@NIP and Mg,N-CDs by 100 ng mL−1 TC and its analogues. (C) The effect of analogues (200 ng mL−1) on the binding of TC (40 ng mL−1) to the Mg,N-CDs@MIP. Influence of coexisting common ions (400 ng mL−1) (D) and other molecules (400 ng mL−1) (E) on the fluorescence quenching efficiency of Mg,N-CDs@MIP in the presence of TC (40 ng mL−1).

(A) Fluorescence responses of the Mg,N-CDs@MIP toward different concentrations of TC. (B) Comparison of the Stern-Volmer plots for Mg,N-CDs@MIP with that for Mg,N-CDs@NIP. (Mg,N-CDs@MIP and Mg,N-CDs@NIP: 0.25 mg mL−1; excitation wavelength: 350 nm; emission wavelength: 465 nm; PBS buffer: pH 7.0; TC concentrations: 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80,100 ng mL-1). The effect of irradiation time at 350 nm (C) and storage time (D) on the fluorescence intensity of the Mg,N-CDs@MIP. (E) The reusability of Mg,N-CDs@MIP for the determination of TC.
Determination of TC in animal-derived food samples by the proposed Mg,N-CDs@MIP and HPLC-UV.

head.ce:title: A strong blue fluorescent nanoprobe based on Mg/N co-doped carbon dots coupled with molecularly imprinted polymer for ultrasensitive and highly selective detection of tetracycline in animal-derived foods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin, 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: a State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health, School of Medicine, Nankai University, Tianjin, 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: b Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health, School of Medicine, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin, 300457, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preparation process of Mg,N-CDs@MIP and the process of FL detecting trace tetracycline in animal-derived foods.

Tetracycline (TC) plays a considerable role in the fight against microbial diseases. However, over-use of TC can produce serious harmful effects on humans and even may lead to drug tolerance. Herein, we developed a novel sensitive fluorescent nanosensor based on the encapsulation of magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Mg,N-CDs) into molecularly imprinted polymer (Mg,N-CDs@MIP), which can be utilized for the specific recognition and ultrasensitive detection of TC in animal-derived foods. The high luminescent carbon dots with a maximum emission wavelength of 455 nm under 350 nm excitation and a 33.05 % quantum yield were firstly synthesized using the Mg/N co-doped fluorescence-enhanced strategy, and the introduction of Mg,N-CDs as a signal tag can significantly enhance the sensitivity of fluorescent sensor. Then, via a facile and efficient one-pot reaction, the Mg,N-CDs@MIP was obtained. The resulting Mg,N-CDs@MIP not only displayed superior optical stability and good reusability, but also exhibited higher specificity and selectivity to TC compared with non-imprinted polymer (Mg,N-CDs@NIP) and Mg,N-CDs. The developed sensor was applied for determination of TC, and its fluorescence intensity was linearly quenched with the increasing concentration of TC from 5–100 ng mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.79 ng mL−1. Additionally, the proposed sensor was utilized for trace analysis of TC in complex foods, and the satisfactory results obtained were consistent with those obtained by HPLC.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC), as a broad-spectrum bacteriostatic agent, has been increasingly used in animal husbandry to promote animal growth and prevent animal diseases [1]. TC plays an important role in the development of animal husbandry, which is also recognized and affirmed by society. However, in recent years, the excessive and continuous utilization of TC has caused residue in animal-derived foods (e.g. milk, meat, egg products) and pollution of water environment [2]. Whether residual tetracycline is continuously accumulated in various aquatic organisms or in livestock, it will eventually enter the human body through human consumption and further lead to a series of adverse effects, including the influence on the development of teeth, the damage to liver and kidney [3], as well as the reduction of human immunity [4]. At present, many conventional analytical methods including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5], high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [6], and immunoassay [7] have been applied to quantitative determination of TC in samples. Based on the complexity of sample preparation and matrix interference, it is imperative to develop fast, inexpensive, succinct and efficient approaches for monitoring trace tetracycline in animal-derived foods.

Fluorescence-based detection method as a sensitive and noninvasive technology has attracted widespread attentions. Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial with a size of less than 10 nm [8]. Compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, CDs exhibit excellent application potential in chemo- and bio-sensing [9,10], bioimaging [11] and drug delivery [12] owing to its prominent advantages including low toxicity, good biocompatibility, high photostability, tunable fluorescence (FL) emission and environmental friendliness [13]. However, CDs are still challenging in the field of FL sensing because of relatively low fluorescence quantum yields (FLQY). As we all know, the fluorescence efficiency of carbon dots is related to its surface state. Surface-passivated and doping of heteroatom (e.g. oxygen, nitrogen, phosphorus, sulfur, and boron) as surface modification nanotechnologies play significant role in improving the fluorescence properties of carbon dots [14]. Heteroatom doping is more efficient and simpler compared to surface passivation [15]. The doped heteroatom can not only tune the unique electronic and optical properties of CDs, resulting in the higher FL efficiency, but also create active sites or abundant functional groups to enhance the affinity of CDs for analytes [16]. For instance, N-doped carbon quantum dots with a quantum yield of 16.2 % were synthesized by a one-step pyrolysis treatment and utilized for the fluorescent assay of chromium (VI) [17]. B-doped carbon dots with a FLQY of 9% were fabricated using ascorbic acid, boric acid and sodium tetraborate as carbon and boron sources [18]. In addition, the CDs doped with dual-elements have more fascinating fluorescence performance. Wang et al. synthesized the S and N co-doped carbon dots with a high FLQY (21.08 %) and good oil-solubility through a convenient solvothermal method [19]. Peng et al. reported a magnesium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Mg,N-CDs), and the FLQY of the prepared carbon dots was as high as 33 % due to the synergistic effect between nitrogen and magnesium [20]. Among these doped CDs, the Mg,N-CDs exhibited superior optical properties (e.g. high photostability and quantum yield). Thus, fluorescent material Mg,N-CDs can be considered as a promising candidate in the filed of analysis applications. Although CDs-based probes have a sensitive fluorescence response to the analyte, the detection systems are still faced with interference from coexisting substances owing to non-specificity of fluorescent probes.

Molecular imprinting technology originated from the principle of antibody and antigen specific recognition in biological immunology and was applied to prepare molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) [21,22]. Similar to natural recognition elements such as enzymes, antibodies and hormones, MIPs possess satisfactory recognition capability and strong affinity for target molecular due to the existence of binding sites complementary to targets [23]. However, under harsh conditions, the artificially synthesized MIPs have higher thermal and chemical stability, as well as superior anti-interference ability than natural receptors [24]. Based on outstanding performance, MIPs are paid increasing attention and have been utilized to construct separation [25], sensing [26] and catalytic platform [27]. Up to now, there have been several researches on the determination of tetracycline based on molecularly imprinted fluorescent nanoparticles [28,29]. In these works, the fluorescent nanocomposite sensors integrated the merits from molecularly imprinted polymers and fluorescence analysis provide a facile, precise and rapid method for monitoring TC molecule in food. However, the reported methods have the problem of low sensitivity, which limits their wide application in the field of optical sensing. Therefore, the exploration of novel sensitive fluorescent nanoparticles is highly desirable.

Inspired by these important studies, a novel ultra-sensitive fluorescent probe (Mg,N-CDs@MIP) for the specific identification and quantitative analysis of trace tetracycline in animal-derived foods was constructed via the facile encapsulation of strong blue Mg,N-CDs into MIPs. In this study, Mg,N-CDs emitted extremely bright blue light were first synthesized by the hydrothermal method as the fluorophore. Then, the carbon dots-embedded molecularly imprinted polymer (Mg,N-CDs@MIP) was fabricated in mixed solutions containing Mg,N-CDs, TC (template), methacrylic acid (functional monomer) and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (cross-linker) through a one-pot strategy. The obtained Mg,N-CDs@MIP probe displayed highly sensitive and selective to TC molecular because of the integration of the unique optical performance of Mg,N-CDs and the superior recognition ability of MIP. Additionally, the preparation and characterization of Mg,N-CDs@MIP were described and discussed in detail, and the proposed probe with good performance was applied to the determination of TC in animal-derived foodstuff.

Tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), chloroamphenicol (CAP) and enrofloxacin (ENR) were obtained from Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). KCl, NaCl, Na2HPO4·12H2O, MgCl2, Zn(NO3)2, CaCl2, FeCl3, glucose, sucrose, anhydrous Citric acid, ethylenediamine and magnesium hydroxide were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. L-Cysteine (L-Cys), L-Arginine (L-Arg), L-Serine (L-Ser), Tyrosine (Tyr), L-Phenylalanine (L-Phe) and L-Histidine (L-His) were provided by Shanghai Yingxin Laboratory Equipment Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), 2,2-azobis (2-isobutyronitrile) (AIBN) and methacrylic acid (MAA) were purchased from TCI Development Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultra-pure water were obtained from a MilliQ purification system (Millipore, USA).

N and Mg co-doped carbon quantum dots (Mg,N-CDs) were synthesized by the reported hydrothermal method [30]. Typically, 10 g of citric acid was first dissolved in 40 mL of ultra-pure water, and then 4.2 g of Mg(OH)2 and 5 mL of ethylenediamine were slowly added to form a homogeneous solution with constant stirring. The mixed solution was then transferred to an autoclave and heated to 200℃ for 3 h. After cooling, the brown-yellow product was further purified by dialysis and lyophilization to obtain Mg,N-CDs powder. Finally, the powder was sealed and stored at 4℃. To consider the effect of Mg/N double doping strategy, N-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CDs) were prepared in accordance with the described above method without adding magnesium hydroxide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Mg,N-CDs@MIP and Mg,N-CDs@NIP composites
ce:section.ce:para: Mg,N-CDs@MIP was fabricated according to the modified one-pot strategy [31]. 3 mg of as-synthesized Mg,N-CDs were dispersed in round bottom flask containing 5 mL of methanol/ultra-pure water (v:v, 9:1) by sonication. Then, TC (0.1 mmol), MAA (0.8 mmol), EGDMA (4 mmol), AIBN (12 mg) were added to the flask one by one and dispersed uniformly under sonication. Next, the oxygen in the system was removed by nitrogen blowing, and the flask was submerged in a water bath and heated to 60 °C for 20 h. Subsequently, the TC imprinted composite was obtained through drying, grinding, and sieving. And the composite was washed with a mixture of acetic acid/methanol (v/v, 1/9) for 48 h to extract TC. Finally, Mg,N-CDs@MIP was obtained by drying at 40 °C for 24 h. For comparison, the non-imprinted polymer (Mg,N-CDs@NIP) was prepared following the identical conditions in the absence of TC. In the meantime, N-doped carbon quantum dots-embedded molecularly imprinted polymer (N-CDs@MIP) was also synthesized following the same procedure as Mg,N-CDs@MIP.

Absorbance spectra were measured on UV-3600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The absolute quantum yields were determined on FLS1000 Edinburgh fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK,) with an integrating sphere. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected with a Tensor-37 FTIR spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany) to analyze function groups of the as-synthesized materials. The morphologies of carbon dots and the polymers were analyzed by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, LEO, Germany), respectively. The thermal stability of the materials was evaluated via thermal gravity analysis (TGA, TA, USA) at a heating rate of 10℃/min to 600℃.

The fluorescent measurements were carried out with a Lumina FL-4500 (Thermo, USA) under the following conditions: the excitation wavelength of 350 nm, excitation/emission slit widths of 10 nm and photomultiplier tube voltage of 700 V. 1 mg of Mg,N-CDs@MIP or Mg,N-CDs@NIP were added into 4 mL of PBS buffer (pH = 7.0) with different concentrations of TC. After incubating for 40 min, FL emission spectra were recorded.

In order to assess feasibility and accuracy of FL detection method established in this work, animal-derived foods (milk, eggs and pork) purchased from local supermarket were selected as the representative samples, and the spiked recovery experiments were implemented. The pre-treatment procedure was designed according to the literature with slight modification [32]. The detailed extraction process was as follows.

The actual samples after homogenization were accurately weighed 5 g and added into the 50 mL centrifuge tubes containing different amounts of tetracycline (25 ng g−1, 50 ng g−1, 75 ng g−1). The samples were mixed evenly and placed overnight. Subsequently, the target molecule TC was extracted twice using 20 mL of Na2EDTA-Mcllvaine buffer solution. After centrifugation (10000 r min-1, 10 min), the twice supernatants of samples were collected, mixed, and then fixed in volume to 25 mL with phosphate buffer to use as an extraction solution. But the pork sample need to be treated with n-hexane to remove fat before the volume was fixed. Finally, 10 mL of the above extraction solution was blown dry with nitrogen, then dispersed in 2 mL of phosphate buffer for FL analysis.

For high-performance liquid chromatography detection, the pre-treatment process and sample analysis procedures were designed and implemented in accordance with the Chinese National Standard (GB/T 21317-2007).

Mg,N-CDs with the highly luminescent and excellent water dispersity (Scheme 1 A) were synthesized by the hydrothermal method based on citric acid (carbon source), ethylenediamine (nitrogen source) and magnesium hydroxide as the precursors. It can be seen in the illustration that the prepared Mg,N-CDs emitted extremely bright blue light under UV excitation (λex = 365 nm). As one of the most important parameters for evaluating fluorescent material, quantum yield (QY) was measured with FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Table S1). Encouragingly, compared with QY of N-CD (14.10 %), the as prepared Mg, N-CDs exhibited a higher fluorescence quantum yield (33.05 %), revealing that Mg/N double doping strategy can efficiently increase the FL intensity of carbon dots. The reason for Mg,N-CDs possessing higher the fluorescence intensity can be explained from two aspects. First, as we all know, nitrogen as a common doping heteroatom was introduced, which can enhance FL efficiency of Mg,N-CDs by altering the electronic and optical properties of carbon dots and creating more active sites/functional groups (amino groups) [16]. Besides, the chelation effect of metal ions (Mg2+) and citric acid contributed to avoid excessive consumption of carboxyl groups (citric acid) in the carbon dot preparation process, thereby improving the fluorescence properties of Mg,N-CDs [20].
The prepared carbon dots Mg,N-CDs were further explored through TEM, FT-IR, EDS, FL and UV–vis analysis in the Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor
ce:section.ce:para: As displayed in scheme 1B, Mg,N-CDs embedded fluorescent biomimetic sensor was constructed through the one-step polymerization strategy. The polymerization process was conducted in a mixed system including Mg,N-CDs (signal reporter), TC (template), MAA (functional monomer) EGDMA (cross-linker) and AIBN (radical initiator) via the thermally initiation in a water bath. Finally, Mg,N-CDs@MIP with the recognition cavities fitting the shape of target molecular was obtained by eluting with acetic acid/methanol (v/v, 1/9) solution. Herein, TC molecule was introduced into the imprinted layer through the hydrogen bond between it and the functional monomer, and the highly luminescent Mg,N-CDs was embedded in the network structure of imprinted polymers owing to its ultra-small size. Furthermore, in order to obtain the polymer with superior FL response and adsorption capacity, the synthesis conditions including the molar ratio of TC to MAA, the molar ratio of TC to EGDMA, the addition amount of Mg,N-CDs and reaction time were optimized in the Supplementary materials (Table S2-S5).

The morphologies of Mg,N-CDs@MIP and Mg,N-CDs@NIP were observed by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 (A–D), both polymers were rough spherical with an average particle size of 350 nm. Apparently, the particle size of the polymers was larger than that of Mg,N-CDs (Fig S1), which meant that the polymers (Mg,N-CDs@MIP and Mg,N-CDs@NIP) were formed. The FT-IR spectra of Mg,N-CDs@MIP after extraction of TC (b) and Mg,N-CDs@NIP (a) were illustrated in Fig. 1E. For curve a, the characteristic peaks at 3608 and 3416 cm−1 (−OH), 2993 and 2949 cm−1 (CH), 1731 cm −1 (CO), 1458 cm −1 (CC), 1259 and 1159 cm-1 (COC) represented the existence of cross-linker (EGDMA) and functional monomer (MAA), and some characteristic vibration peaks (1392 and 853 cm-1) related to Mg,N-CDs appeared in curve a, which illustrated the formation of non-imprint polymers doped with Mg,N-CDs. In addition, curve b was not significantly different from curve a in the position and intensity of the characteristic peaks. The above FT-IR results proved that Mg,N-CDs@MIP probe was successfully prepared. The TGA analysis was performed under a flow of nitrogen to investigate the thermal stability of synthetic materials (Mg,N-CDs, Mg,N-CDs@MIP, and Mg,N-CDs@NIP). The results of weight change with temperature were depicted in Fig. 1F. For the TGA curve of Mg,N-CDs, the first-stage weight loss from 30℃ to 100℃ was approximately 10 %, corresponding to the absorbed water in material. During 100−500℃, the weight loss was about 52.1 % owing to its decomposition. As can be observed from curves of Mg,N-CDs@MIP and Mg,N-CDs@NIP, the weight loss of them were about 97.7 % and 97.1 % and primarily occurred at 250−450℃, deriving from the decomposition of polymers. Compared to pure Mg,N-CDs, the downward trend of Mg,N-CDs@MIP curve was basically consistent with that of Mg,N-CDs@NIP curve, and there was almost no weight loss at 30−250℃, which revealed that the polymers (Mg,N-CDs@MIP and Mg,N-CDs@NIP) possessed highly thermal stability.

The optical performance of Mg,N-CDs@MIP was explored. As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, Mg,N-CDs@MIP demonstrated a symmetric fluorescence emission at 465 nm under 350 nm excitation. There was some red-shift in the emission spectrum of Mg,N-CDs@MIP compared to that of Mg,N-CDs. As is well known to us, the fluorescence of Mg,N-CDs is related to its surface state. During the bonding process, template, monomer, and crosslinker may cause changes to the surface of Mg,N-CDs (such as protonation or deprotonation) [29], and the modification of imprinting layer enabled the diameter of Mg,N-CDs to enlarge obviously, which led to a red-shift in fluorescence emission of Mg,N-CDs@MIP [33]. Evidently, the FL intensity of Mg,N-CDs@MIP was reduced compared to that of Mg,N-CDs (Fig. 2A). However, the results in Table S1 demonstrated that the FLQY of Mg,N-CDs@MIP (21.4 %) was significantly higher than that of N-CDs@MIP (10.12 %), which meant that the as prepared Mg,N-CDs@MIP maintained the remarkable fluorescence performance.

The pH (3–12) of the solution had a noteworthy influence on the FL intensity and FL quenching efficiency (F0/F) of Mg,N-CDs@MIP probe due to the protonation and deprotonation process. As displayed in Fig. 2B, the FL intensity of probe dramatically increased in the pH range of 3–7, and then decreased slightly with a further increases of pH values from 7 to 12. Evidently, the strongest FL signal of probe was observed at pH = 7, and the highest FL quenching efficiency was simultaneously acquired. Thus, the neutral condition (pH = 7) was chosen for further experimental.

Meanwhile, the sensing mechanism of imprinted polymer (Mg,N-CDs@MIP) for TC molecule were studied. As depicted in Fig. 2C, the FL intensity of Mg,N-CDs@MIP was effectively quenched when there was TC molecule in the imprinted cavities. However, after removing the target molecule with an appropriate elution solution (acetic acid/methanol), the FL intensity of Mg,N-CDs@MIP was restored to 91.4 % of Mg,N-CDs@NIP. The above fluorescence results confirmed that the prepared Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor can effectively remove and recombine target molecules under appropriate conditions, implying that the sensor had good feasibility for detecting TC. Moreover, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching was discussed and analyzed in Fig. 2D. Evidently, there was almost no overlap between the UV absorption peaks of TC and FL emission spectrum of Mg,N-CDs@MIP, excluding the possibility of energy transfer. However, comparing the excitation spectrum of Mg,N-CDs@MIP and UV absorption spectrum of TC, there was a distinct overlap between the two spectra. So the inner filter effect (IFE) can be considered as a possible mechanism to explain the fluorescent quenching [34,35]. The fluorescence lifetime of Mg,N-CDs@MIP was 7.66 ns (Fig. S5). After combining with TC, the fluorescence lifetime of the sensing system remained almost unchanged (7.32 ns), which further certificated that the quenching behavior of TC to the fluorescence of Mg,N-CDs@MIP was due to IFE rather than FRET. Furthermore, the UV absorption peaks of TC molecule and Mg,N-CDs were close. Therefore, when TC was adsorbed and bound to the imprinted layer, the excited electron in the conduction band of Mg,N-CDs can be transferred to the lowest unoccupied orbital of the TC molecule, which resulted the FL quenching of sensor (Mg,N-CDs@MIP) [36]. From the above analysis, the fluorescence quenching was ascribed to the synergistic effect of charge transfer and IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption behavior of Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor for TC
ce:section.ce:para: In order to confirm that the proposed Mg,N-CDs@MIP probe has superior fluorescence performance and binding ability to the template TC, we conducted an in-depth investigation.

First, the adsorption ability of Mg,N-CDs@MIP and N-CDs@MIP toward TC molecular was investigated by exposing the polymers to a series of different concentrations of TC. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, the FL quenching degree (F0/F) of polymers increased dramatically with the increase of TC concentration ranging 20−100 ng mL−1. However, Mg,N-CDs@MIP exhibited higher FL quenching efficiency than N-CDs@MIP at the same TC concentration. The reason for above phenomenon was that Mg,N-CD as the fluorophore enabled Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor to possess more excellent optical properties (e.g. high quantum yield) and exhibit a higher FL response for TC molecule, resulting in a signal amplification.

Next, adsorption dynamic experiment was carried out by monitoring the fluorescence response (F0/F) of polymers (Mg,N-CDs@MIP, Mg,N-CDs@NIP and N-CDs@MIP) at different contact times, and the results were described in the Fig. 3B. Within 40 min, the fluorescence response of polymers increased with the extension of adsorption time. After that, the F0/F value of polymers remained with no remarkable change, which implied that the adsorption of polymers towards TC molecule reached equilibrium. Evidently, among these polymers, the F0/F value of Mg,N-CDs@MIP was the largest under the same conditions. In other words, compared with N-CDs@MIP and Mg,N-CDs@NIP, Mg,N-CDs@MIP had higher FL response and more outstanding binding capacity to target molecule, which was ascribed to the introduction of ultra-sensitive Mg,N-CDs and the presence of high-affinity TC-imprinted sites in Mg,N-CDs@MIP. Additionally, 40 min was chosen as the optimal adsorption time in subsequent experiments.

Besides, the affinity of as-prepared composite probes (Mg,N-CDs@MIP and Mg,N-CDs@NIP) towards TC molecule was further evaluated by monitoring the UV–vis absorption spectra of the TC solutions adsorbed by Mg,N-CDs@MIP and Mg,N-CDs@NIP, respectively. As revealed in Fig. S6, the non-adsorbed TC in the supernatant of Mg,N-CDs@MIP was significantly less than that in the supernatant of Mg,N-CDs@NIP under the same conditions, which meant that Mg,N-CDs@MIP possessed stronger affinity towards TC. In fact, the main reason for the above phenomenon was that there were many imprinting cavities and active binding sites in the imprinting layer, which enabled Mg,N-CDs@MIP probe to exhibit superior affinity and binding capacity towards TC. On the contrary, since there were no target molecules during the polymerization process, the obtained Mg,N-CDs@NIP without imprinting cavities can only absorb a small amount of TC molecules. The above results further demonstrated that the RBV-imprinted cavities can effectively enhance the Mg,N-CDs@MIP probe's affinity and adsorption capacity for TC. So the developed Mg,N-CDs@MIP composite probe with high affinity can be utilized for efficient recognition and determination of TC.

The binding selectivity of the proposed Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor for TC was assessed by selecting structurally related compounds including oxytetracycline (OTC), chlortetracycline (CTC), chloroamphenicol (CAP) and enrofloxacin (ENR) as interfering substances, and the chemical structures of TC and its analogues were drawn in Fig. 4 A. As shown in Fig. 4B, the FL quenching effect of TC on Mg,N-CDs was significantly higher than that of the analogues, confirming the fact that the prepared N and Mg co-doped carbon quantum dots shown a higher FL response for tetracycline antibiotics (CTC, OTC and CTC). Fortunately, the modification of imprinting layer can effectively overcome the interference of coexisting antibiotics with similar structure on TC detection. As expected, Mg,N-CDs@MIP displayed higher FL response toward TC than other analytes (CTC, OTC, CAP and ENR), which was ascribed to the specific binding sites created by molecular imprinting (Fig. 4B). However, there were no recognition sites that were completely complementary to the size, shape and functional group of CTC, OTC, CAP and ENR in Mg,N-CDs@MIP, so it is difficult to capture these analogs. Noticeably, compared with Mg,N-CDs@MIP, the fluorescence change of Mg,N-CDs@NIP was relatively weak in the solutions containing TC or the analogs, because Mg,N-CDs@NIP bound to various analytes through non-specific physical adsorption. The above results revealed that imprinting technology as an important means enabled the constructed Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor to possess superior specificity and selectivity.

Meanwhile, competitive adsorption experiments were implemented by adding different analogs (CTC, OTC, CAP and ENR) into the TC-containing solution (40 ng mL−1) to evaluate the resistance of Mg,N-CDs@MIP to interference from analogues, and the addition amount of each analog was 200 ng mL−1. As displayed in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence response of Mg,N-CDs@MIP remained basically unchanged even if the concentration of analogs was 5-fold that of TC, which demonstrated that the interference generated by analogs was very slight. This phenomenon may be due to the FL selectivity that came from Mg,N-CDs and the specificity of TC-imprinted cavities. Thence, the prepared Mg,N-CDs@MIP exhibited stronger FL response and higher affinity to template TC during adsorption.

Besides, some potential interfering species were bring by sample pre-treatment, including common metal ions (Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, PO4 3− etc.), amino acids (L-Cysteine, L-Arginine, L-Serine, Tyrosine, L-Phenylalanine, L-Histidine) and saccharides (glucose and sucrose). In order to further verify the specificity of prepared sensor in complex environment, these interfering substances were also investigated. The interference study was designed in the presence of each co-existing substance. The Fig. 4D and E demonstrated that these substances in samples caused only tiny fluorescence variation even at a concentration up to 400 ng mL-1 (10-fold of TC).

According to the results mentioned above, we can draw the conclusion that the synergistic effect of Mg,N-CDs and TC-imprinted sites endowed Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor satisfactory sensitivity and outstanding anti-interference ability. Therefore, the constructed Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor was identified as an ideal fluorescent material for efficient determination of TC in complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of fluorescence sensing detection method
ce:section.ce:para:
The dose response of fluorescent materials (Mg,N-CDs@MIP and Mg,N-CDs@NIP) for TC was evaluated by adding different concentrations of TC solution. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 A, the FL intensity of Mg,N-CDs@MIP was gradually quenched along with the increase of TC concentration. Compared with Mg,N-CDs@NIP (Fig. S7), Mg,N-CDs@MIP displayed higher the quenching efficiency at the identical TC concentration. Furthermore, the relationship between the fluorescence response (F0/F) and quencher (TC) concentration was depicted in Fig. 5B and analyzed using the Stern–Volmer equation.
F
0 /F = K
SV
C
TC + 1

In which F0 and F represented the FL intensity of sensing probe before and after binding to TC, respectively, Ksv was the Stern–Volmer constant, and CTC was the concentration of TC. Herein, imprinting factor (IF) was defined as the ratio of Ksv,Mg,N-CDs@MIP and Ksv,Mg,N-CDs@NIP, and was used as an important indicator to assess binding ability of Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor. As revealed in Fig. 5B, the F0 /F value of Mg,N-CDs@MIP was higher than that of Mg,N-CDs@NIP at the same quencher dose, which was caused by the absence of TC-imprinted cavities in Mg,N-CDs@NIP. The corresponding standard linear equation of Mg,N-CDs@MIP was F0 /F = 0.0028 CTC + 1.0087, and the linear correlation coefficient R 2 was 0.9976. The linear range of the sensor covered 5–100 ng mL−1, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.79 ng mL-1 which was calculated following the standard 3σ criteria (3σ/S) [34,37,38], where σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution, and S is the slope of linear calibration curve. The calculated IF was 3.76 under the most favorable conditions, which meant that Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor had an outstanding specific recognition capacity to tetracycline.

The optical stability of CDs-based sensor (Mg,N-CDs@MIP), as a crucial parameter, was evaluated by recording the fluorescence intensity changes against exposure time, storage time. The results, as shown in Fig. 5C, indicated that the obtained sensor can endure continuous UV irradiation (350 nm). There was no remarkable change by comparing the 7-times fluorescent data within 1 h, illustrating that the sensor possessed desirable anti-photobleaching property. After preserved at 4 °C for six weeks (Fig. 5D), Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor maintained a stable fluorescence emission, which meant that the sensor had superior photostability.

In order to appraise the reusability of developed sensor, six removal-rebinding cycles were undertaken, and the FL intensity of Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor was monitored during the process (Fig. 5E). Briefly, the Mg,N-CDs@MIP nanoparticles (10 mg) were added into 40 mL of PBS buffer containing TC (100 ng mL−1) to form mixture solution with three parallel groups. Firstly, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture was measured. After adsorption equilibrium, the fluorescence intensity was recorded again. Next, Mg,N-CDs@MIP nanoparticles were washed with acetic acid/methanol (v/v, 1/9) solution, and then collected by centrifugation. Evidently, as the number of reuses increased (Fig. 5E), the fluorescence intensity of Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor was slightly decreased, because it is difficult to remove the TC molecules remaining in the inner regions. However, the sensor retained 93.5 % of its original fluorescence intensity after six cycles. highlighting the good reusability of Mg,N-CDs@MIP. Furthermore, the above results shown that constructed sensor can be reused at least 6 times without a significant decrease in the measurement capacity for TC molecule.

The applicability of proposed fluorescent sensing method was discussed and analyzed in animal-derived foods by comparison with traditional detection method (HPLC-UV). Milk, eggs and pork were taken as real samples in this study. Since the three blank samples did not find TC using the above method (FL and HPLC method), the spiking and recovery experiments were carried out by adding the different concentrations of TC (25, 50 and 75 ng g−1). The detection was repeated three times at the same concentration, and the corresponding recovery rate and relative standard deviation (RSD) of the sample solution were calculated. In addition, the chromatograms of TC standard solution and spiked samples were provided in the supplementary material. As shown in Figure S8, no significant interference was observed at the retention time of TC, indicating that matrix interference can be ignored. As displayed in Table 1 , the recoveries of samples acquired by utilizing Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor were between 78.6 % and 98.7 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was in the range of 3.3−5.0. Evidently, the results of FL detection were highly consistent with that of HPLC-UV. Thus, the developed Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor provided a well-pleasing platform for quantitative detection of TC in food samples.

The established fluorescent sensing method based on Mg,N-CDs@MIP was compared with some detection methods that have been developed so far. Obviously, the developed fluorescent method in this work displayed wide dynamic range, good precision (related to the standard deviation, RSD), comparable recovery and a lower detection limit (Table S6). Herein, the introduction of imprinting technology endowed Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor with higher thermal and chemical stability and superior anti-interference ability, which was contributed to avoid the complicated pre-processing and shorten the analysis time. The Mg,N-CDs with a high quantum yield were embedded as a signal tag, which significantly enhanced the sensitivity of the Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor. Additionally, fluorescence-based sensing method were easy to operate. In summary, the FL sensing method based on Mg,N-CDs@MIP was more suitable and promising for the rapid determination of trace tetracycline in complex samples.

In this research, a novel Mg,N-CDs embedded fluorescent biomimetic sensor was developed to detect TC through the synergistic effect of the charge transfer and inner filter effect. Thanks to the unique fluorescence properties of N and Mg double-doped carbon quantum dots, the as prepared Mg,N-CDs@MIP exhibited more significant fluorescence response to trace TC. In addition, the introduction of imprinting sites endowed Mg,N-CDs@MIP sensor favorable selectivity and desirable recognition ability. Thence, the combination of imprinting technique and FL detection improved the selectivity and sensitivity of the sensor. The proposed probe finally was applied to the analysis of TC in animal-derived samples with satisfactory performance, and the results obtained were consistent with the traditional HPLC method. Therefore, Mg,N-CDs@MIP had enormous potential in future food inspection.
Xuelian Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Writing-Original draft preparation.
Yueqing Zhao: Data curation, Writing-Reviewing.
Jingyuan Dong: Writing-Reviewing.
Chang Liu: Investigation, Validation.
Ying Qi: Validation.
Guozhen Fang: Supervision.
Shuo Wang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author(s) declare that they have no competing interests.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129809.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Analyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.M. Committee, Recommendations for the Definition, Estimation and Use of the Detection Limit, Analyst. 112 (1987)

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xuelian Hu is currently a doctor course student in Department of Food Science, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her researches mainly focus on the development and application of MIP-based fluorescent sensors.
Yueqing Zhao has received her master degree in Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her researches mainly focus on the development and application of MIP-based fluorescent sensors.

Jingyuan Dong is currently a master course student in Department of Food Science, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her researches mainly focus on the development and application of fluorescent sensors.

Chang Liu is currently a doctor course student in Department of Food Science, Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her researches mainly focus on the application of high-performance liquid chromatography analysis in food.
Ying Qi has received his master degree in Tianjin University of Science and Technology. His researches mainly focus on the application of high-performance liquid chromatography analysis in food.

Guozhen Fang received her Ph.D. in analytical chemistry in 2005 at Nankai University, Tianjin, China. Now she is serving as a professor in Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her researches mainly focus on the molecular imprinting technique and instrumental analysis, also their relative application in the detection of food and environmental contaminants.

Shuo Wang received his Ph.D. in agricultural chemistry in 1999 at University of Sydney, now he is serving as a specially appointed professor in Nankai University. He has long been engaged in the research fields of Food Safety and Food Science.
